commentBody: Fast and fun.

The clue for FAKE NEWS should be “when media is accused of misinforming “. Just because someone calls news “fake” doesn’t mean it IS fake. Hard Pass on seeing this in the puzzle.

SARAH Palin, ABORT, FAKENEWS, RAMBO, HARAM, RASH decisions....

I feel like the puzzle is calling me to make a joke that Barry won't find funny.

Archaeopteryx  comes from archaeo (old) AND pteryx (wing), thus pronounced ARCHEO  TERREX

DALL-E doesn't produce art. It produces a stolen amalgam of artist's work.

This is a reaction to the Midi crossword puzzle : No, DALL-E and other AI tools cannot create art. They can create images, rip-off artists by mimicking their style, but you can't call that art.

Now back to my insomnia (first step of a repatriation move tomorrow, and we haven't finished packing everything)

FOIL has appeared 76 times as an answer, defined not only as the fencing weapon, but as metal sheeting, a sidekick, to thwart, etc.  EPEE has appeared almost ten times as often (756, to be exact), always clued as the sword (because how else?)  According to Occam's razor, EPEE is the horse, FOIL the zebra.  I would have thrown EPEE in without a second thought, and dealt with it later if it had been wrong.

The shape of the Greek letter Φ (PHI) is one I know well, mostly because my buddy Andrzej likes to use it to substitute for a foul word that begins with the sound of PHI.

As for the "Friday Night Lights" answer goes, the given year should make it clear that it's about the movie; the TV show ran multiple years.

Congrats on your well earned debut, Max. This puzzle was fun but ANTIPERSPIRANT (no sweat).
My only nit is that the HEADLESSHORSEMAN—though dead, supernatural, and headless—is still a he not an IT. I am disappointed that we are dehumanizing the cranially challenged and I am certain that Nearly Headless Nick from Hogwarts would be equally aghast. After all they are just trying to get ahead in the world.
Fun puzzle! However, would have preferred TECHIES or TECHBROS instead of just TECHS for computer geeks.
HOOHA made me laugh. Ruckus is not what I use that word to describe. :-o

OH HEY there, Max's best friend, hello and....surprise! Yes, it's Max's debut puzzle in the NYT! And isn't it superb? (Some would say it's fire, or it's lit, but that belongs to yesterday's puzzle and comments. Old NEWS. Plus, it's not befitting of me because I'm in the "cool" age group.) Yes, this was a very cool puzzle, what with the theme being in the clues. It jolted my brain a bit, messing with expectations, and that's a great thing. Another something that messed with my HEAD was the clue [How much 1990s music was issued.] I just couldn't make sense of it, thinking *what amount* of 1990s music was issued. What do you mean? –How many songs? –How many albums? And how am I supposed to know? ON CD. Ohhh, _that_ kind of "how much." Head slap. And speaking of music, my last entry was the G of GO TO. Without the G, tOTO was where my mind went. So here's a blast from the past: https://youtu.be/qmOLtTGvsbM?si=XMcPfid0lQ7w7uwJ (Toto with "Rosanna") Mr. Schlenker, congratulations on your NYT debut, respect for your persistence and resilience, and many thanks for a terrific Tuesday puzzle!
I’m so glad to see other people pushing back on the Midi. AI is absolutely not capable of creating art!
Bravo, Max!  A fun puzzle with clever clues was just what I needed today. Loved the clues for HEADLESSHORSEMAN and LADYFINGER. Looking forward to your next one!

commentBody:
Righteous debut.

Mad persistence.

Anyone else annoyed with the new app on iOS? I like that the comments are now in the games app, but to get from the comments back to the puzzle, I have to tap top left of my screen, followed by bottom left, followed by top right. Can’t the back button be in the same corner on each page?
Sorry NYT, AI cannot create art. It can steal it, copy it, and destroy it, but it does not create. Do better
That's the clue you picked for NOSES

Loved the clue "It's a no-brainer." I know I'm a millennial because TOTES unwillingly made me flash back to that commercial with Malcolm McDowell and James Earl Jones, like a character with PTSD in a police procedural.

KETTLES was my fav. This was a fun theme!

It’s not rocket science
PAPER AIRPLANES

It’s not brain surgery
OPERATION 

It’s not that deep
KIDDIE POOL

As ever, Go Gunners! 🏆🏆 
GOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOAL!

The NE corner was impossible for me, even though my wife and I drove through Boise so I knew IDAHO, and I came up with ODESSA with a few crosses. Going for fArmANIMALS, being completely stumped by the biker clue, not knowing SKEE-ball or that TECHS could mean computer geeks, and never having heard of NOSES being used to take attendance all combined to be my undoing. I needed reveals to get out of that bind. As for the theme... Was there one even? I mean... On Friday these would just be considered somewhat misdirecting clues. Now for some cultural differences. Our schools often have sport teams, but they almost never have mascots - actually, team mascots are generally not a thing over here, in any league of any sport, that I know of. Our schools also don't do pep rallies - they sure did not 20-40 years ago when I was at school/university. "Olimpiada" is what we call Olympic games. In a school context, "olimpiada" is a regional or national competition for the best students in a given subject. Winning one lets you skip some final exams in elementary/high school and get into high school or university more easily. [Nerdy flex] As a nerd son of professors of history, I was a laureate of the national history Olimpiada, which got me into Warsaw University's law school without exams [/nerdy flex] And now for something completely different - here is Lucyfer the Poodle blending in with my in-laws' carpet: https://imgur.com/a/J1grAUf
The fact this article continues to open in the NYT games app and not the main app continued to be a pain in my rear

Fun fact: The name "Arby's" does not derive from "Roast Beef", but rather "Raffel Brothers" (Forrest and Leroy) who founded it in 1964.

Fun fact #2: A word like "emcee" is called a lexicalized initialism—an abbreviation that’s been turned back into a normal-looking word based on how it sounds. Other examples: okay, deejay, kayo.

I saw the MIDI answer early on, but refused to believe that actual journalists would consider AI slop to be art. It’s stolen intelectual property. It’s an amalgamation of countless real pieces of art. But it in itself is NOT art.

So, I can handle the Monday thing.

One particular entry today made me think of one of my all-time favorite NYT XW puzzles, from Thursday, 2007/III/01, by Steven Atwood (a debut). Which I solved in my first round of regular solving, roughly 2000-2010, in print. Here is the link: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2007/03/01 (Finding this required some creative use of the search functions of xwordinfo.com.) For whatever reason, I did not find today's puzzle to be a piece of cake, a walk in the park, etc. (for a Tuesday). But now I want lady-fingers. Tiramisù is all well and good, but when was the last time you had a good serving of Charlotte Russe?

Glad to see I'm not the only one who chafed at 20A.

But those are close enough for crossword clues. If they were more rigorous they would often be too easy.

Nice debut! The theme was cute, and there was plenty of lively fill: OLYMPIAD, RAMBO, DATUM, HOOHA.

Whenever I think of the HEADLESS HORSEMAN I think of that old Disney movie. I just looked it up, and apparently it was some sort of mash-up of The Legend of Sleepy Hollow and The Wind in the Willows? I only remember the Ichabod part.

For "It's a no-brainer", I at that point only had HEAD and I wanted either HEAD FULL OF STRAW ("Wizard of Oz" reference) or HEAD FULL OF MUSH ("The Paper Chase" reference.)  When HEADLESS HORSEMAN rode -- most unwelcome -- into my grid, I cried "Foul!"  HH is a "He"; he's not an "It".  If you see what I mean.

Otherwise a cute theme with cute clues for the theme answers.  And a second puzzle in a row with no junk -- though I do sometimes wonder what puzzle creators would do if the IKEA store had never been invented.

I found the clues and answers to this one a bit hackneyed.

My initial attempt at solving this puzzle resulted in maybe 20% completion, filling in 18A and 73A along with a few other "Piece of Cake", or "no Brainer" answers. My initial thought was a “chICK puzzle,” but I quickly dismissed that thought. (Too soon?)

Speaking of "Thy will be done," here’s an interesting observation I made during this first year as a Wordplay reader and reluctant participant:

Let’s consider the NYC time zone as a reference. It could be noted that there are roughly three or four distinct categories of commenters, similar to shifts in a factory. These could be labeled as Days, Swings, and Mids, or First, Second, and Third. I won't venture out a fourth category. While the specific labels don’t matter, the underlying principle is the same.

The early commenters, who are the ones who comment shortly after the puzzle is released, hoo boy! Depending on where you are in the world, quite the way to start your day, or to end it.🤨

P.S.
Oh, only NOW do I find out that some clues were in italics!  That would certainly have helped!  Given that there are 142 clues, sorting through them to find the theme was too much to expect of  this old solver!  A note/notice would have been nice.

Very tidy and enjoyable Tuesday puzzle. I never realised how many metaphors there are for an easy task/decision. 

I personally don’t see a problem with the clue for FAKE NEWS. “Misinformation” is a synonym for fake, and “media” is a form of news circulation. Whilst I get that people are saying the term has taken on a new meaning due to its misuse by right wingers, it’s literal definition has not changed. We can’t start changing language simply because some of us are too stupid to use it correctly. 

I hold more umbrage for how much NEATO is appearing lately, however. Makes me feel like I’m living in a 50s sitcom. Can we please give this one a breather for a bit? Would be much appreciated.

Not related to the main puzzle…

On today’s Midi puzzle in the clue about DALL-E, you misspelled “steal”.

A couple of comments about the Midi. 

Concerning 2D:  One thing cannot be “a million times smaller” than another any more than one thing can cost 600% less than another. A million times any positive number is always more than the number. And size is always positive.

And just a picky comment about the clue for 1D: The word DRESS doesn’t add anything, except possibly an image in one’s mind.

I find it odd how some folks seem to think the NYT Crossword Puzzle needs to reflect their personal opinions.

FAKENEWS is an all-too-common contemporary phrase for, yes, media misinformation. It doesn't matter if most accusations of such are accurate or not, nor from where on the political spectrum such accusations originate. The term's fair game and the clue is fine.

ART, like many words, is polysemous. It means Da Vinci and Warhol and my daughter's preschool crayon scrawlings and advertising layouts (and even The Deal). What do these A.I. programs produce, then, 'graphics'? (Yes, I know  'slop'.) OK, it's not 'Fine Art', but it's a kind of art, in a way. Clue is fine.

Lighten up, is my personal opinion.

Because this is a nitpicky group, allow me to point out the correct use of the word “factoid” would be to indicate a statement which is fact-like (hence the suffix -oid, meaning “resembling”). An example might be, “it takes 10,000 hours to become an expert.” In other words, a statement that seems true but can’t be substantiated,

I believe you were using factoid to mean “trivia” or small fact. Perhaps we should start using factlet for that?

FAKE NEWS in the NYT? Perish the thought.

Unsolved mysteries: did the HEADLESS HORSEMAN carve his own jack o' lanterns, or just steal them off of someone's porch? Okay, that was just in the Disney version - in the original story, it was just a pumpkin. But the question remains; could the vengeful spectre only ride in October? Did he even need the pumpkin, or was that Brom Bones' creative license? Hmm, I have "Sleepy Hollow" on DVD, might have to watch it again.

Much as I might like to agree with those who say AI can't produce art, we should note that Christie's sold an AI-generated portrait for $400K almost 10 years ago. To settle the question definitively, I asked AI, and it said we're all engaged in a "category panic, not analysis." The market has settled the issue, it declares. So there we have it, from the horse's mouth.
Love the clue for 60D. Congrats on the debut!

Fun fast Tuesday. Congrats Max on your debut. If your friend spots your byline today I hope you'll stop by to let us know. Media misinformation?  C’mon.  How about “Trump’s misdirection”?  I’d use a a more vulgar word, but I have my doubts you’ll even approve the way I phrased this comment.

Today I have decided to take umbrage regarding the East Coast bias of the clue "General Tso's Chicken" (in some form or other ). 
This bit of crosswordese assumes that those of us on the better coast have ever seen the item on a menu. Ha! and I say again: ha. 
Been to many Chinese restaurants on the West Coast, never seen it. 
Further research reveals that General Tso's Chicken was invented in the 1950s and imported to the U.S. in 1973.  And, this research (admittedly a grand name Googling it and glancing at a Nat Geo headline) reveals indeed that it is primarily an East Coast dish. 
The General and his troops never managed to invade the West Coast -- which is no doubt due to the Rockies being difficult to surmount with elephants.

Editors, please get the definition of your medieval weaponry straight.
Maces are blunt, possibly with flanges
A morning star has spikes on the end
A flail is a stick or mace connected by something flexible like a chain to a spiked end.

Big BOOOOOOOOO to FAKENEWS

I have a question about the Math OLYMPIAD: when I was in high school, I was on the team for Olympics of the Mind. Apparently, the IOC took umbrage, and the name of the competition was changed to Odyssey of the Mind. Does the IOC no longer have a stranglehold on the term and its variants?

My usual late puzzle find. A Sunday from August 28, 1977 by Maura B. Jacobson with the title: "City Additions." I won't list any clues, just some of the answers: TAIWANONTODAY BLACKCOALOFCALCUTTA ARRIVIDERCIAROMA RHODESCOLLAR LEARNTOPARKDAKAR GETYOURBERINGSTRAIT TOKYOROES ROTOMANDALAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=8/28/1977&g=136&d=A ....
A thoroughly meh puzzle

More ruminations on the FAKENEWS.

We recently had a flare up in this forum about a vulgar word being used as an answer in the puzzle.

It didn't upset me at all, but it upset some people here who I like and respect very much.

I argued that they are words, just words, merely words.  SBK (I think) wrote a humdinger of a response about the power of words, which was really compelling, and a masterful demonstration of the power of words.

I couldn't disagree with even a single sentence of it, so I had to do what I could to make the two ideas fit.

I think the key thing here is context.  It is absolutely certain that words gain meaning and power from their context, "...of the people, by the people, and for the people"..., or "...life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness"..., or "blessed are the peacemakers"... 

I find these enormously powerful words, but it's because they are held up by the words around it, riding on their shoulders.

But the context of a crossword is as insanely precise as it is limited.  Each word or phrase exists there *only because every letter is perfectly placed* relative to letters of other, unconnected words around it.

So, FAKENEWS, and other words like them, doesn't (or shouldn't) raise my dander as an answer in a puzzle, as it might have when used in a sentence.

And that is my two cents.

"Fake news" originally meant actual fake news stories, often from sites that purported to be satire in the fine print but were mainly just priors-affirming rage bait designed to go viral (this is the sense it's used in the clue). Only in 2016 did it become slang for legitimate journalism that one particular person didn't like, and like many other things, this new meaning took over to an extent that many people don't even remember the original meaning.
All, There are some ugly sentiments a few comments before this one. Suggest you ask moderators to remove

Are you familiar with the expression "false equivalence"?

My brother-in-law was trying to tell me the other day that the recent, unauthorized and sudden destruction of one-third of the White House was the same as Barack Obama installing a basketball court, or Harry Truman a bowling alley, in the White House.  Presidents have the right to make alterations to the White House, he said...

The same people call the NYT a "leftist newspaper" because it's left of the bat-guano crazy stuff coming out of rightist media, and if the latter is all you listen to, that's your world view. @Steve L
The heartbreaking part is that people with whom you share many parts of your life go plunging into the Valley of Lies, never to be seen again. 
Oh sure, they're physically present at the family dinner or the movie night or even if you go for coffee. But they're hollowed out and the person you loved or had as a lifelong friend isn't there.
And it's hard to converse when you have to plan every sentence to avoid the pitfalls. Weather? Um, no -- climate change. Sports? With all those people of indeterminate or just plain wrong genders competing?! Nuh-uh. As for sharing confidences or talking out your troubles: it can't be done.
It's no joke.  😔

I do have a brother who also drank the Kool-Aid, but we've never been that close, and he lives in Texas these days.  I don't see him much, but to be honest, he was never that deep a thinker. @LarryF
It seems reasonably clear that the Greeks pronounced the pi in "ptero-"  even if we don't.

@LarryF Nope. In English we don’t say the p at the start of words when it’s in a cluster (ph excluded since thats just the way we write Greek phi). But medially we pronounce it. So psychology starts with an /s/ and Ptolemy with a /t/, but optical and epsilon have a /p/.

@Ιασων "Greek pronunciation of diphthongs αι ευ ει οι αυ and double consonants μπ (today’s OLYMPIAD) but sometimes (depending on placement) making the sound ‘b’ , γγ for ‘g’, γκ for a different ‘g’, ντ for ‘d’ can be confusing."

That's only for Modern Greek, not (presumed, reconstructed) Ancient Greek.

@LarryF
Anyone who doesn't pronounce the "p" in "pterodactyl" is just being lazy!

(Current paleo-linguistic research suggests that the pterosaurs themselves dropped the initial "p" about 66.000.000 BCE, and look where it got them!)

(God, I love this thread!)

@Bill the longest documented continuously in use language (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greek_language) has obviously evolved. I can only tell you how we speak it now. Feel free to speak it as you wish. I also take exception to the poor spelling of American English so I think it’s fair 😀
@SBK in TO Αγκώνας — elbow — indeed sounds ‘ng’
@Bill 😀

@Ιασων
Oh Dear! I hope I didn't come off as judgemental--that was hardly my intention. My knowledge of Modern Greek pronunciation is limited to that of a non-speaker's, amateur linguist's understanding of it--which is to say, not much. Still, it's hard to see a word like "beer" spelled as "Μπύρα," and not see it in my mind's eye as "mpyra."

Vowels in particular--as an English speaker, I'm so aware that the quality of vowels have drifted so far from their original pronunciations, yet still I find it hard to realize that that process has happening in many other languages, Greek included. 

Back to Greek--in early loans from Greek into Old Latin, words spelled with an "ύ" were transliterated with a "u"; in Classical Latin, with a "y"; and still later, with an "i," as the Greek vowel became more forward, and less rounded.

The Italian-American linguist Mario Pei made an interesting illustration--in Greek, the sound a sheep makes is written as "βή-βή," which, in Ancient Greek would have been pronounced "bay-bay," whereas in Modern Greek "vee-vee" (sort of?--please confirm!). The sheep, however, have not changed.

@John
I studied the Russian language, and it has many words, some very common ones, that start with consonant clusters,  i.e.
book: kniga
where: gd'e
who: kto

Lots of beginning students have trouble with this, and I remember teachers pointing out that we don't have a problem saying them in the middle of words--i.e.
jacknife
Ogden
actor

languages are cool.
@Bill all good 👍😀 this is part of the fun of discussing here

@Ιασων Thanks. I've found a reference site here: https://atticgreek.org/pronunc/gammaU.html This note confirms my memory and even uses the same example -- angelos is spelled with a double-gamma but is free of any visible nus

@Marie

@Marie Is there a philosophical argument that in fact that is all human artists can do as well? Just Devil's advocating for a sec to see what people think... Of course I'd prefer a piece of art from a talented and well-rewarded human any day.

@Andrzej Nope, moving back to France from the German speaking part of Switzerland. To be fair though, the job market has been horrendous since the whole Credit Suisse debacle. I wouldn't judge anyone making the choice to repat to the US, unemployment benefits only cover 1.5-2 years and as foreigners we are not eligible for social help even after 10+ years living here.

@Alex

First, a disclaimer: I am horrified by AI infringing on people's intellectual property and replacing creativity with mere reproduction, even more so as I am an author myself. Intellectual theft is discouraging legitimate creators, and slop is replacing works of real value. Still...

I understand your point Alex, I think. What is the difference between any random person and an artist? And what makes using AI to create images (or other forms of artistic expression) different from relying on any other technique?

And this gets even more complicated - some artists employ AI in their creative process on purpose. Now, how to differentiate between an *artist* and a non-artist in this context? Is public recognition as an artist what makes a person one?

When I prompt an AI to create an image with a theme I came up with, the theme remains mine - ultimately the image is about me expressing myself, and AI becomes my tool, only.

@Alex I would argue that I never produced art when I was copying existing images. I don't know how to properly explain it (blame it on the aforementioned insomnia) but there is an aspect of intent behind art, and AI can't have actual intent. I'd say similarly, when Temu is copying of small designers, are they producing fashion or soulless, intentless version of a human craft? 
That being said, if you like video games, Detroit Become Human could be an interesting play. It was released in 2018 but I feel like our discussion fits into the theme.

@Alex
Same for journalists and comedians. Why cant a niche blogger with a small following or some random funny dude be considered each, respectively?

@Andrzej I think a lot of expats are feeling desperate right now and find the idea of moving back to their families in the US is comforting. In expat groups you would find on a daily basis people asking if it would be so bad to repat. My partner is non-EU and this move is hard, there are tons of barriers everywhere and after all these years in Switzerland I would understand if he gave up on expatriation.

@Marie

That was a great game, wasn't it? Games rarely move me, but that one sure did, much like "Heavy Rain" from the same developers. I wish there were more empathetic and narrative-driven games like this at present...

I’m quite fascinated by this topic.

My mother says that if art and music 
can “become pre-printed greeting store cards”
then they have no value.

I don’t buy Hallmark cards,
but many do and are quite touched by the sentiment 
that was printed on the card when their loved one bought it.

In art school, design and textile classes included
the use of stencils and reproduced patterns.

I’m not chiming in with attitude.
I am certainly watching this new age unfold
and imagining its ugliness and its beauty.

@Andrzej That was the first game I played with my partner, it was a great way to get to know each other at the ethics level,. 
There is unfortunately a real lack of risk taken by devs/publishers, quite similar to the movie industry. Last game I played was the last Life is Strange, it was disappointing to see how even a franchise that was groundbreaking when it started is now just doing fan service.

Ain't it funny how our heads work? I also remember a great many things from childhood, but I already forgot the name of the person I met last week...

As to the matter at hand: why would one count noses, specifically? Heads or raised hands are so much more natural.

Historically, people paid taxes in Poland per household or per person. The former tax was called "podymne" - "dym" means "smoke," and since the whole household shared a house, which came with one fireplace/oven and one chimney, each household produced one "dym". The latter tax was "pogłówne" - "głowa" means "head," and "pogłówne" roughly translates as "per head."

Now, we never had a "ponosne" (per nose) tax 🤣

A friend recently gifted me with a bunch of french baking equipment, to add to the hoard of French baking equipment I've collected over the years--enough to open a small patisserie, which I might, were I younger, and had more energy, capital, and state-provided health care. One piece was a beautiful stainless steel charlotte mold; I have yet to use it.

A variation is the Charlotte Royale, for which the lady-fingers are replace by slices of (jelly- or Swiss) cake roll. It's usually molded in dome.

Editorial question--is it proper, in English, to capitalize recipe names? In the case of Charlotte Russe, both "Charlotte" and "Russe" are proper nouns (sort of), but what about "Tiramisù," or "Pappardelle with Pork, Fennel, and Lemon Ragù Bianco"?

When I saw "three...averages", what came to mind were the harmonic, geometric, and arithmetic means.   Of course none of the adjective parts fit, so I guess the author wanted the noun  part, but then why the "one of"?   Perhaps the author was thinking of the triplet arithmetic mean, median, and mode, which are often discussed together.   Technically these are all "statistics", i.e., functions of a data set, with others being standard deviation, skewness, and kurtosis.

@Rich in Atlanta As promised - a Sunday from February 3, 2008 by Matt Ginsberg with the title: "Just Follow Directions." Not easy to describe this one, but here are some examples: 11a -Clue: "Pineapple Desserts" Answer: UPSIDEC But the 'C' was in an un-numbered square and the down answer there was: CAKES. So the implied answer was... UPSIDE(DOWN)CAKES. Another example: 54d - "Quite wrong" Answer: OUTINF But then the across squares running into the F were... DLEIF, so the implied answer to 54d was... OUTIN(LEFT)FIELD Some other combination answers with the implied direction in parentheses: PICKED(UP)SPEED so... PICKEDS crossing the down answer DEEPS And some other combination crossing answers: EXTREME (RIGHT) WING ALL (RIGHT) ALREADY SETTING (UP) SHOP STAND (UP) COMIC You have to see it to really get it. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/3/2008&g=54&d=D ...
